one_liner: A 26-year-old African American woman presents with 2 months of fatigue, joint pains, and a facial rash that worsens after being in the sun. She also reports painless oral sores and pleuritic chest pain.

stage2.hpi_extension: She describes migratory arthralgias involving the small joints of her hands, wrists, and knees with mild morning stiffness. A red rash across her cheeks and nose sparing the nasolabial folds flares whenever she spends time outdoors. She has several painless oral ulcers on the hard palate. Over the last week she has had sharp chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration. Review of systems is notable for hair thinning, Raynaud phenomenon, and foamy urine.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol. Sexually active with one partner.
stage2.physical_exam: General: mildly ill-appearing young woman. Skin: erythematous malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds; no scaling. Oral: two painless ulcers on the hard palate. Joints: tender MCPs and wrists without frank deformity or erosive changes. Cardiac: pericardial friction rub audible at the left lower sternal border. Lungs: mild pleural rub at the right base. Extremities: trace pedal edema. No livedo reticularis.

lab_results.result: ANA positive at 1:1280 (homogeneous pattern). Anti-dsDNA antibodies markedly elevated. Anti-Smith antibodies positive. Complement C3 and C4 low. Anti-phospholipid antibodies pending.
lab_results.clinical_significance: ANA is highly sensitive (>95%) but not specific. Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are highly specific for SLE; anti-dsDNA titers correlate with disease activity, particularly lupus nephritis. Low complement reflects active immune complex consumption during a flare.

lab_results.result: Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (normocytic), WBC 3.2 x 10^9/L (lymphopenia), platelets 98 x 10^9/L (mild thrombocytopenia).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Cytopenias involving multiple cell lines are common in SLE and are a classification criterion. Autoimmune hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, lymphopenia, and thrombocytopenia can all occur.

lab_results.result: 2+ proteinuria, red cell casts, and dysmorphic red blood cells.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Active urinary sediment with red cell casts indicates glomerulonephritis, concerning for lupus nephritis and requiring renal biopsy to define the WHO class.

stage5.final_clue: Renal biopsy shows diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis (class IV lupus nephritis) with wire-loop capillary lesions. She is started on high-dose corticosteroids, mycophenolate mofetil, and hydroxychloroquine.

debrief.classic_presentation: SLE classically affects young women of African American, Hispanic, or Asian descent (female to male ratio ~9:1) and presents with a constellation of findings summarized by the mnemonic SOAP BRAIN MD: Serositis, Oral ulcers, Arthritis, Photosensitivity, Blood disorders (cytopenias), Renal involvement, ANA, Immunologic (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm), Neurologic, Malar rash, Discoid rash. Constitutional symptoms such as fatigue, fever, and weight loss are common.
debrief.pathophysiology: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease characterized by loss of tolerance to nuclear self-antigens, leading to autoantibody production and deposition of immune complexes (type III hypersensitivity) in tissues such as skin, joints, kidneys, and serosal surfaces. Complement is consumed during immune complex formation, producing low C3 and C4 during active disease. There is a genetic predisposition (HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3) and environmental triggers include UV light, EBV infection, and certain drugs.
debrief.treatment_overview: All patients should receive hydroxychloroquine, which reduces flares and mortality (monitor for retinal toxicity). NSAIDs and low-dose steroids control mild disease. Moderate-to-severe flares and lupus nephritis require high-dose corticosteroids plus immunosuppressants such as mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide. Belimumab (anti-BAFF) is approved for refractory disease. Strict sun protection is essential.
high_yield_pearls: Anti-dsDNA antibodies correlate with disease activity and lupus nephritis; anti-Smith antibodies are highly specific but do not correlate with activity.
Drug-induced lupus (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid) is associated with anti-histone antibodies and usually spares the kidneys and CNS.
Antiphospholipid antibodies in SLE cause arterial and venous thromboses, recurrent miscarriage, and a false-positive VDRL/RPR.
Libman-Sacks endocarditis = sterile, non-bacterial verrucous vegetations on both sides of the mitral valve in SLE.
Complement levels (C3 and C4) are low during SLE flares due to immune complex consumption.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Systemic lupus erythematosus' section.
